metadata.title: Comprehensive AIPDF Schema Feature Showcase
metadata.subtitle: All Supported Block Types, Attributes, and Structures in One Document
metadata.author: AIPDF Team
Open Semantic Systems

section.title: Introduction to AIPDF
section.paragraph: The AIPDF format is an AI-native PDF extension that embeds a Brotli-compressed semantic XML layer as an associated file inside a valid PDF document. Legacy PDF readers ignore the embedded payload and display the visual content stream. AI-aware parsers extract the structure directly without OCR.

The core invariant is simple: the PDF is the visual authority and the embedded XML is the machine-structure authority. Nothing is duplicated between the two representations. This keeps the semantic layer minimal and efficient.
section.note: This document exercises every element and attribute defined in the AIPDF 1.0 schema to validate the full rendering and extraction pipeline across all SDK surfaces.

section.title: Design Goals
section.paragraph: The format was designed around four non-negotiable requirements that must all hold simultaneously:

list.item: Semantic preservation without OCR or heuristic extraction
Full backward compatibility with legacy PDF 1.7 readers
Minimal size overhead via Brotli compression (target under 10%)
Universal AI parser support across all major SDK surfaces
Security hardening against prompt injection and DOCTYPE attacks

section.paragraph: These goals are achieved through a carefully layered architecture: scraper-based HTML normalization, quick-xml structural validation, brotli stream compression, and hand-crafted PDF byte assembly using only base-14 fonts.

section.title: Supported Input Formats
section.paragraph: The AIPDF toolchain accepts four source formats, each converted through a dedicated normalizer to canonical semantic XML before embedding:

list.item: XML — direct schema-validated input, no conversion required
Markdown — heading-based section detection with paragraph blocks
HTML — scraper DOM walk covering all block-level elements
Typst — heading syntax mapping to Markdown normalizer

section.title: System Architecture
section.paragraph: The architecture consists of three layers: source input normalization, semantic XML validation and compression, and PDF assembly with embedded payload. Each layer has a well-defined interface and can be exercised independently through the public API.

image.alt: AIPDF system architecture: source input normalization through semantic XML validation to PDF assembly with embedded Brotli-compressed payload

figure.caption: Figure 1: The AIPDF processing pipeline from raw source input to the final .aipdf file. Each stage has a clearly defined interface: source.rs handles normalization, security.rs handles sanitization, xml.rs handles validation, and pdf.rs or render.rs handles final assembly.

row.cell: HTML, Markdown, Typst to XML conversion
XML to Markdown and MDAST JSON export
Columnar ONTO export for LLM ingestion

section.title: Data Flow
section.paragraph: Every input passes through the same six-stage pipeline regardless of source format. This ensures consistent security properties and validation guarantees across all entry points.
section.codeBlock: Input (XML / MD / HTML / Typst) -> source::semantic_xml_from_source -> security::sanitize_xml -> xml::validate_xml -> pdf::brotli_compress -> render::build_rendered_pdf (Full mode) -> .aipdf file
section.annotation: The data flow is strictly unidirectional. No stage has feedback into a prior stage. This makes the pipeline fully testable at each boundary.

section.title: Render Modes
section.paragraph: Two render modes are available. Minimal mode produces a compact 9-object PDF with plain text visible content. Full mode activates the production layout engine with styled headings, tables, code blocks, and multi-page flow.

section.title: Compression Mathematics
section.paragraph: The compression efficiency of the semantic layer can be expressed as a dimensionless ratio comparing the compressed payload size to the original XML size. Higher efficiency means lower overhead in the final PDF file.

definitionList.definition: Compression efficiency as a percentage (0 to 100)
Raw UTF-8 XML byte count before compression

section.paragraph: For typical document XML, Brotli at level 6 achieves efficiency values between 70 and 85 percent, meaning the compressed payload is 15 to 30 percent of the original XML size. This translates to a PDF overhead of 3 to 10 percent for most document sizes above 10 KB.
section.footnote: Brotli level 6 is used as the default. Level 11 achieves 5 to 8 percent higher compression at the cost of significantly longer encode times. For interactive document builders, level 6 is the correct tradeoff.
The 16 MiB size limit on semantic XML payloads is a security invariant, not a practical constraint. Real-world documents are typically under 1 MiB of raw XML.

section.title: Security Model
section.paragraph: The security model is designed to prevent three attack classes: prompt injection via the semantic layer, PDF action exploitation, and denial-of-service via oversized payloads. Each is handled at a different layer of the pipeline.
section.note: Security sanitization runs on every XML path — both at build time (before embedding) and at extraction time (after decompression). This provides defense in depth: a tampered .aipdf file cannot inject unsanitized content into the extraction pipeline.

section.title: Disallowed Markers
section.paragraph: The sanitize_xml function performs a case-insensitive substring search for each disallowed marker. Any match causes the entire document to be rejected with an error. The following markers are blocked:

list.item: PDF JS action type (prevent script execution via PDF actions)
PDF Launch action type (prevent arbitrary process execution)
LLM injection prefix strings (prevent prompt hijacking)
AI instruction override patterns (prevent AI system manipulation)

section.annotation: The marker list is conservative by design. False positives (legitimate documents rejected) are preferable to false negatives (malicious documents accepted). Document authors who need to discuss these terms should use character entity escaping or reformulation.

section.title: Size Limits
section.paragraph: A hard 16 MiB limit on the uncompressed XML payload prevents memory exhaustion attacks. The limit is checked after decompression to catch compressed bombs. Decompression itself is performed with a 4 KiB streaming buffer to avoid large intermediate allocations.

section.title: SDK Reference
section.paragraph: Three SDK surfaces expose the same semantic API: the Rust core library plus CLI, the Python SDK, and the TypeScript/npm package. All three implement the same output shape for reading order, Markdown export, and ONTO export.

section.title: Rust Core Library
section.paragraph: The Rust library is the reference implementation. All other SDKs are ports of its logic. The core public API exposes document building, extraction, inspection, validation, and four export functions.
section.note: The CLI wraps the Rust library with a thin clap-based argument parser. It adds no logic of its own — every command maps directly to one or two library calls.

section.title: Python SDK
section.paragraph: The Python SDK is a pure-Python implementation depending only on the brotli package (version 1.1.0 or later). HTML conversion uses the stdlib html.parser module with no external dependencies. The ONTO encoder mirrors the Rust scalar encoding rules exactly.
section.codeBlock:
import aipdf
doc = aipdf.open("document.aipdf") print(doc.to_markdown()) print(doc.to_onto())
# Build from various sources from aipdf import build_from_source xml = build_from_source(html_content, "html") xml = build_from_source(md_content, "markdown") xml = build_from_source(typ_content, "typst")

section.title: TypeScript SDK
section.paragraph: The TypeScript SDK is an ESM module with zero runtime dependencies. It uses Node's built-in zlib module for Brotli decompression. HTML and Markdown conversion use regex-based tokenization. The package is tested with Vitest and published to npm.
section.annotation: The TypeScript SDK targets Node 18+ for the built-in Brotli support. Browser environments require a Brotli polyfill since the Web Crypto API does not include Brotli decompression.

section.title: Export Formats
section.paragraph: The semantic layer can be exported in four representations, each optimized for a different downstream consumer. All four formats are available through the CLI, Python SDK, and TypeScript SDK.
section.citation: PDF 2.0 (ISO 32000-2) associated files provide the embedding mechanism used for the semantic payload. The EmbeddedFile stream uses the custom subtype marker /application#aipdf+xml+br to distinguish it from other associated files a PDF may contain.

The XMP metadata stream embedded in the PDF includes AIPDF-namespace fields: Version, SemanticFile, SemanticEncoding, ContentAuthority, OCRPolicy, and byte counts. These allow AI systems to detect the semantic layer without performing a full byte scan.

section.title: Glossary
section.paragraph: Key terms used throughout this specification and the AIPDF codebase:

definitionList.definition: AI-native PDF extension format. A valid PDF 1.7 file with a Brotli-compressed semantic XML payload embedded as an associated file.
The embedded aipdf-semantic.xml.br associated file containing the machine-readable document structure. Absent in plain PDFs; present in all .aipdf files.
A PDF 2.0 concept (also backported to PDF 1.7 via the AF array) for files embedded inside a PDF and logically associated with its content.
A columnar, token-efficient export representation for LLM ingestion. Emits Document, Sections, Blocks, Tables, Figures, and References as tab-separated records.
An enum with two variants: Minimal (9-object hand-crafted PDF, default) and Full (production layout engine with multi-page flow and styled elements).
A struct representing a single semantic unit extracted from the XML: title, paragraph, caption, equation, citation, cell, item, codeBlock, reference, footnote, or note.
The security gate function that rejects DOCTYPE declarations, inline scripts, prompt injection strings, and other disallowed markers from XML payloads before validation or compression.

references.reference: ISO 32000-2:2020 — Document management — Portable Document Format — Part 2: PDF 2.0. International Organization for Standardization, Geneva, 2020.
Alakuijala, J., van Asseldonk, R., Rubin, S., Kliuchnikov, I., Obryk, R., Szabadka, Z., and Vandevenne, L. Brotli: A General-Purpose Data Compressor. ACM Transactions on the Web, 13(1), 2019.

Peterson, D., Gao, S., Malhotra, A., Sperberg-McQueen, C. M., Thompson, H. S., and Biron, P. V. (Eds.) W3C XML Schema Definition Language (XSD) 1.1 Part 1: Structures. W3C Recommendation, April 5, 2012.
Alakuijala, J. and Szabadka, Z. Brotli Compressed Data Format. RFC 7932. IETF, July 2016.
Felber, J. quick-xml: High performance XML reader and writer. Rust crate. https://crates.io/crates/quick-xml
Webb, C. scraper: HTML parsing and querying with CSS selectors. Rust crate. https://crates.io/crates/scraper
